segments.s268-g217: بفرمود is an honorific past-tense verb meaning 'he ordered/said' (third person). تا here introduces the subordinate clause (sense 'so that/that'), marking what was commanded. نامه پاسخ is a noun phrase where نامه 'letter' is modified by پاسخ 'reply', so 'a reply letter'; نوشت is the past form of 'to write' and here completes the subordinate clause ('be written' / 'write'), so the whole chunk means 'he ordered that a reply letter be written.'
segments.s268-g218: درختی is an indefinite object 'a tree'. به نوی is an adverbial phrase made with به + adjective نوی 'new' and yields 'newly' or 'in a new way'. به کینه gives the motive 'for/with hatred' (i.e. 'out of spite'). بکشت is an archaic-past causative/performative form (here 'had ... planted' / 'caused to be done' or simply 'planted/caused to plant'); together: 'he had a tree newly planted out of spite.'
segments.s268-g201: سراپرده نو بپرداختند is ordered as Noun + adjective + verb. سراپرده (compound noun) is the object 'the inner chamber/room' modified by the following adjective نو 'new'. بپرداختند is the past tense, 3rd person plural of پرداختن with the prefix بِ-; here it means 'attended to, furnished or laid out'. The -ند ending marks 'they'.
segments.s268-g202: نشستنگه خسروی ساختند places the place-word نشستنگه 'seat/seat-place' before the adjective/attribute خسروی 'royal/kingly', then the verb ساختند 'they made/built' (past, 3rd pl). خسروی is an adjectival/genitive modifier of نشستنگه; ساختند is the main finite verb referring back to the whole NP.
segments.s268-g203: به دیبای رومی بیاراستند uses به + noun phrase to indicate the material or manner ('in/with Roman brocade'): به here functions like 'in' or 'with' (instrumental). دیبای رومی is a noun (brocade) qualified by the adjective/ethnic rumi 'Roman/Byzantine'. بیاراستند is the past, 3rd-person-plural of آرائیدن/آراستن 'they adorned/arranged'.
segments.s268-g204: خورش‌ها و رامشگران خواستند is a coordinated object phrase (خورش‌ها 'dishes' and رامشگران 'those who soothe/comfort') followed by the past tense verb خواستند 'they desired/asked for'. The plural marker -ها on خورش shows plurality; و simply links the two nouns; خواستند is 3rd person plural past.
segments.s268-g205: پراندیشه گشته دل پهلوان places the predicate 'پراندیشه گشته' ('has become full of thought/worried') before the noun 'دل پهلوان' ('the hero's heart'), a common classical inversion. گشته is the perfect participle/auxiliary form of شدن used here to mean 'has become' (past/result state); پراندیشه is an adjective or adjectival compound meaning 'full of thought/worry'.
segments.s268-g206: نبشته ابا رای‌زن موبدان is a nominal phrase where نبشته functions as 'the written [text/letter]'; ابا is used adverbially/nominative to indicate reluctance or refusal (i.e. 'reluctantly' or 'with reluctance'); رای‌زن موبدان is a postnominal agent phrase 'the mobeds who give counsel' (رای‌زن modifying موبدان). Together: 'the written [response], reluctantly, by the counselors among the mobeds.'
segments.s268-g207: همی پاسخ‌نامه آراستند has the adverb همی (classical connective/adverb, here signalling 'thus/thereupon' or a continuative 'then') followed by the noun پاسخ‌نامه 'response letter' as direct object and the past plural verb آراستند 'they arranged/prepared'. آراستند is 3rd person plural past of آراستن/آراییدن.
segments.s268-g208: سخن هرچ نیکوتر آن خواستند literally orders as 'speech/words (سخن) — the more good/ better (هرچ نیکوتر) — that (آن) — they wished/asked (خواستند)'. هرچ نیکوتر is a comparative/intensifying phrase meaning 'the better (it could be)' or 'as good as possible'; آن is a demonstrative referring back to the wording; خواستند is past 3rd plural 'they wanted/required'.
segments.s268-g73: وز آن‌سو که شد رستم گرد سوز is a relative/time clause: وز (an archaic connective here, effectively ‘and/on’) + آن‌سو ‘that side’ introduces a place; که شد ‘where/when [he] became’ is a relative clause marker + past verb (شد = became); رستم is the proper name; گرد سوز is a poetic nominal phrase meaning ‘a whirl/whirlwind of burning’ (گرد as a dust/whirl image with سوز ‘burning/heat’). The clause sets the scene for what follows.
segments.s268-g74: سپارم بدو کشور نیمروز is the main clause: سپارم is a first‑person present/jussive form ‘I entrust / I commit’; بدو ‘to him’ is the indirect object; کشور نیمروز ‘the country Nimruz’ is the direct object (noun phrase with head کشور and name نیمروز). Word order here places the verb before its objects in a poetically compressed line.
segments.s268-g75: ز کوه و ز هامون بخوانم سپاه uses repeated poetic prepositions ز ‘from’ (ز کوه ‘from the mountain’ and ز هامون ‘from the plain’) followed by بخوانم ‘I call/summon’ (present/jussive of خواندن here meaning ‘to call up, summon’) and سپاه ‘the army’ as object. The verb precedes its object, a common ordering in classical Persian verse.
segments.s268-g76: سوی باختر برگشاییم راه reads as a goal phrase + verb + object: سوی باختر ‘toward the west’ gives direction, برگشاییم is 1st‑person‑plural jussive/subjunctive ‘we will open / let us open’ (برگشادن = to open), and راه ‘the road/way’ is the thing being opened. Together it means ‘we will open/clear the way toward the west.’
segments.s268-g77: بپردازم این تا در هندوان: پردازم (from پرداختن) is used as ‘I will carry out / accomplish’ (present/jussive used for future/intent), این ‘this’ is the direct object or demonstrative referring to the foregoing action, and تا introduces a purpose/result clause ‘so that / into’ with در هندوان ‘in/among the Indians’. So the clause means ‘I will carry this into the Indian lands / accomplish this among the Indians.’
segments.s268-g78: نداریم تاریک از این پس روان: نداریم ‘we do not have / we shall have no’ (present negative of داشتن), تاریک ‘darkness/dark’ is the predicate, and از این پس ‘from this after / henceforth’ gives the time frame. روان here functions adverbially as ‘onward / henceforth’ (poetic use). The whole means ‘from now on we shall have no darkness.’
segments.s268-g79: ز کشمیر وز کابل و قندهار is a list of places introduced by the poetic preposition ز ‘from’: ز کشمیر ‘from Kashmir’, (وز کابل) here the writer repeats the preposition in the older poetic style to mark ‘and from Kabul’, and و قندهار ‘and Kandahar’. The repetition is typical of classical enumeration.
segments.s268-g80: شما را بود آن همه ز این شمار is a copular/result clause: شما را (with را marking the direct object/topic) بود ‘was’ (past of بودن) + آن همه ‘all those’ + ز این شمار ‘from this count / among these (in this tally)’. Literally: ‘All those were yours in this reckoning.’ The verb بود is past and attributes possession/entitlement.
segments.s268-g1: یکی here is the indefinite 'a certain' placed before نامه (a common poetic idiom). نامه فرمود is a classical collocation meaning 'he issued/sent/caused a letter' with فرمود used in the sense of ordering/causing. پس is a temporal adverb 'then', تا introduces the purpose/result clause ('so that'), and دبیر is the agent (the scribe) of the following verb (implicitly nominative).
segments.s268-g2: نویسد is the present-subjunctive/jussive form 'may/should write' used here after تا to express purpose ('so that he might write'). سوی is the preposition 'toward/to', پهلوان is 'champion/hero' and دلپذیر is an adjective meaning 'pleasing/delightful' modifying پهلوان.
segments.s268-g3: سر نامه 'at the head/top of the letter' (a locative noun phrase). کرد is the verb 'he made/placed' or 'he did' taking آفرین بزرگ ('a great praise/acclamation') as its object; آفرین here is an acclamation/praise rather than the verbal stem.
segments.s268-g4: This is a short prayer/formula: به یزدان پناهش = 'may God be his refuge' (به + divine name + پناه with enclitic -ش 'his'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from', followed by دیو سترگ where دیو 'demon' and سترگ here denotes 'gaze/sight' so the phrase means 'from the demon's gaze' or 'from the gaze of the mighty demon.'
segments.s268-g5: دگر گفت is 'he said again' / 'then he said' (دگر = again/another). کز here introduces a content clause ('that' / 'from which') linking to the following clause; کردگار جهان = 'the Creator of the world' is the source/target of the speaker's request.
segments.s268-g6: بخواهم is first-person present-subjunctive 'I will/would ask or wish' and is the main verb of the content clause introduced by کز. همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('indeed/just' / older continuous sense), and آشکار و نهان are coordinated adverbs/adjectives 'openly and secretly' (parallel objects/complements indicating manner).
segments.s268-g7: مگر introduces an exception/conditional clause 'unless' (or 'except that'). کز again links into the relative/subordinate clause. میان is the preposition 'between', and دو رویه سپاه is 'the two faces/sides/ranks of the army' (رویه here = surface/side, so the whole phrase means 'between the two fronts of the army').
segments.s268-g8: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign/king'; بردارد is a present/subjunctive-ish form meaning 'may take away / should remove' (here 'remove' from the context); این is the demonstrative 'this' modifying کینه‌گاه, which is a compound meaning 'seat/place of hatred' (i.e. the cause or locus of enmity).
segments.s268-g57: The adverbial/source phrase 'از ایران' (from Iran) precedes the directional phrase 'به کوه اندر آید' (into the mountain he comes). 'اندر آید' uses the old/poetic preposition 'اندر' = 'into/in' plus the verb 'آید' (come). In epic narrative 'آید' often functions as a historic present meaning 'came' or 'arrived'; 'نخست' at the end is the temporal adverb 'first/initially'.
segments.s268-g58: 'در' marks location ('in/among'); 'غرچگان' here names the place/people group (a region or people), so it functions as a locative noun. The short phrase 'از بر بوم بست' is idiomatic: 'بر' = 'on/upon' and 'بوم' = 'native soil/home', while 'بست' (from بستن) means 'to fix/establish' (here: to set up or settle). The whole clause therefore says he fixed/established his dwelling there.
segments.s268-g59: 'دگر' introduces another region ('another/next'). 'طالقان شهر' is a genitival/attributive sequence meaning 'the city of Taleqan' (modifier before noun is common in classical Persian). 'تا' here means 'as far as' and links this place to the following toponym 'فاریاب' (Faryab) indicating the extent.
segments.s268-g60: 'همیدون' is a proper place-name. 'در بلخ' gives its location ('in Balkh'). The second 'تا' again means 'as far as/up to' and is followed by 'اندر آب' ('in/into the water'), so the clause states that Hamidun in Balkh extends as far as the water (a river/stream).
segments.s268-g61: 'دگر' again marks another item in the list ('also/another'). 'پنجهیر' is a place-name; 'و در بامیان' coordinates an additional location ('and in Bamiyan') with 'در' marking the locative. Word order is straightforward enumeration of places.
segments.s268-g62: 'سر مرز ایران' is a compound meaning 'the head/top of Iran's border' (سر = top/head; مرز = border; ایران = Iran). The conjunction 'و' links a second characterization 'جای کیان' meaning 'the place/seat of the Kian (Kayanian) kings' (جای = place/seat; کیان = the kings/royal house). The whole phrase characterizes Panjhir/Bamiyan as frontier and royal seat.
segments.s268-g63: 'دگر' introduces yet another locality. 'گوزگانان' (the people/land of Guzgan) names the district; 'فرخنده' is an adjective 'auspicious/fortunate' qualifying it; 'جای' (place) completes the description. The phrase is a nominal description preparing the following verbal clause.
segments.s268-g64: 'نهادست' is an archaic/poetic perfective form meaning 'has been placed' or 'was set' (from نهادن plus the enclitic/old copula). 'نامش' = 'its name' (نام + enclitic ش = its). The following two words 'جهان کدخدای' function as the name given: 'Jahān-Kadkhudāy' (literally 'world–headman' used here as a proper name/title). Overall: 'Its name was set: Jahān-Kadkhudāy.'
segments.s268-g105: بپیوندم and بدوزم are volitive/subjunctive 1st-person forms expressing a wish or resolve ('may I / let me'). This segment lists the direct objects joined by و (and). Word order is noun phrase after the verb (verb — object), a common poetic order.
segments.s268-g106: بدوزم is another 1st-singular subjunctive/volitive 'may I sew/stitch'. به introduces the beneficiary/target ('to/into'), followed by مرکب nouns دستِ وفا and چشمِ کین (the possessive relation is implicit: 'hand of fidelity', 'eye of vengeance'). The sequence places the two targets in juxtaposition without an overt conjunction between them in Persian poetic style.
segments.s268-g107: که here introduces a subordinate temporal/relative clause ('when' / 'which'), followed by بشکست, a past/inchoative verb meaning 'broke/was shattered' used intransitively. هنگام ('at the time of') modifies the verb phrase, and شاه بزرگ ('the great king') is the subject phrase placed after the temporal adjunct in poetic order.
segments.s268-g108: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of' marking cause; بد گوهر is a genitive compound meaning 'evil / ill nature (lit. bad essence)'. تور and سلم are proper names joined by و, and سترگ qualifies سلم as an epithet ('mighty/keen-eyed' — here 'gaze/eye' is meant). The whole phrase gives the cause of the shattering.
segments.s268-g109: This relative clause describes Fereydun: که introduces 'who/that', از marks cause ('from/because of'), درد is 'pain/sorrow', سرگشته is a predicate adjective 'bewildered/distraught', and شد is the past auxiliary/verb 'became' — together 'who became distraught from sorrow.'
segments.s268-g110: کجا here functions as a temporal/locative 'when/where' introducing the circumstance; ایرج نامور is the named victim ('Irāj the renowned'), کشته is the passive participle 'killed/slain', and شد is the past auxiliary forming the passive ('was slain'). The clause locates the event that caused Fereydun's distress.
segments.s268-g111: ز من ('from me') signals the source ('ask of me'), هرچ means 'whatever', باید is the modal 'ought/must', and به نیکی بخواه uses به + noun to form an adverbial phrase 'kindly / in goodness' with بخواه (imperative/subjunctive 'ask/seek'). The clause is an imperative request phrased politely.
segments.s268-g112: از آن پس is a temporal phrase 'after that'; بر این نامه کن is an imperative instruction where بر here means 'on/with/regarding' and کن is the imperative 'do' (with نامه 'this letter' as its object) — idiomatically 'take/bring/execute this letter'. نزد شاه ('before the king' / 'to the king') gives the destination. The sequence gives the next action to perform.
segments.s268-g177: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when/once'; the verb رسید (past) comes at the end as is normal in Persian. رویین (adjective) precedes پیران (noun) forming the phrase 'the iron-clad elder(s)'. به + درگه is the prepositional phrase 'to/toward the court'.
segments.s268-g178: سوی is a directional preposition 'toward'; پهلوان is its complement 'the champion'. سپه کس is a noun + noun compound meaning 'a man of the host/army' (specially, سپه modifies کس). The clause ends with the past verb دوید 'ran', with subject 'سپه کس' placed before the verb.
segments.s268-g179: فرستاده را is the direct object marked by را; خواند is past tense here meaning 'called/summoned' (not 'read'). پس is an adverb 'then' introducing what follows; پهلوان is the noun 'the champion' (topic for the next clause).
segments.s268-g180: دمان appears as a proper name/epithet (not one of the given common senses) and serves as the subject; از پس پرده is the prepositional phrase 'from behind the curtain' (از + پس + پرده); آمد is the past tense 'came'; جوان is a post-verbal apposition/adjective 'young' (here 'a young man') describing the subject.
segments.s268-g181: بیامد is a past-tense/vivid-past 'he came' introducing the action. چو + [گودرز را دید] is a temporal subordinate 'when he saw Goudarz' — گودرز is object marked by را and دید is the past 'saw'. دست (noun) begins the next coordinated action 'hand...' (object/part of an idiom).
segments.s268-g182: دست به کش کرد is an idiomatic verbal phrase: 'to put the hand to (کش) and do (کرد)' — here meaning 'he laid his hand to/drew (his sword)'. کرد is the light/auxiliary past verb. و links the next action: سر پیش بنهاد پست = 'he placed/bent his head forward low' — سر is object, پیش 'forward/before', بنهاد (past of نهاد) 'placed/put' and پست 'low' modifies the manner.
segments.s268-g183: سپهدار (the commander) is the subject; بر جست is a verbal phrase 'sprang up' (بر here gives the sense 'upon/up' and جست is past 'leaped'). و connects the next clause; او را marks 'him' as the direct object of the following verb. چو دود is a simile 'like smoke' modifying how the action was done.
segments.s268-g184: به آغوش تنگ اندر آورد is the main verb group 'brought into a tight embrace': به + آغوش تنگ 'into the narrow/close embrace', اندر آورد (آورد past 'brought') is the verb. زود at the end is an adverb 'quickly/soon', emphasizing the speed of the action.
segments.s268-g33: نباید که is an impersonal negative modal meaning "it ought not / one should not"; که introduces the subordinate clause. زشتی (a noun) stands before the verb and means a bad quality or moral vileness; بماندت is an archaic/poetic 3rd-person singular subjunctive/perfective form of ماندن "to remain/linger" used here to express "remain/be left"; نام is the object meaning "name" in the sense of reputation or fame, so the clause literally says "it should not be that an ugly name remain."
segments.s268-g34: وگر is an archaic conditional "if / but if." تو is the 2nd-person subject "you." بِدان here functions as a prepositional/causal phrase "by that / in that (knowledge or circumstance)" formed from the preposition ب- + دان (used here adverbially); سر شوی is ``سر شدن'' with شوی the 2nd-person singular subjunctive/present of شدن "become" and سر functioning as a stative/result noun ("in heart/head / in station"); شادکام is an adjective meaning "fortunate/joyful," so the clause reads roughly "if you thereby become joyful."
segments.s268-g35: هر آنگه که is a temporal clause marker meaning "whenever / when." موی سیه شد سپید is a compact clause: موی "the hair" + سیه "black" (its former state) + شد (past of شدن "become") + سپید (result adjective, here "white/grey-haired"). Word order places the theme (hair) and its prior color before the verb and the resulting adjective after it.
segments.s268-g36: ببودن is a verbal noun/infinitival form meaning "to be / existence"; نماند is a past/factual negative of ماندن "to remain / be left," here meaning "did not remain / was not left"; فراوان modifies امید meaning "much / abundant hope." Together the phrase means "much hope was no longer left."
segments.s268-g37: بترسم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive of ترسیدن used here as a present-tense narrative "I fear" (literary). که‌گر is a fused conditional particle equivalent to "if"; بار دیگر is a time expression "on another occasion / again"; سپاه is the subject of the following clause "the army."
segments.s268-g38: به جنگ اندر آید is an idiomatic sequence meaning "enter into battle / come to fight": به + جنگ ("to/into battle") + اندر (archaic locative "in/into") + آید (3rd-person singular present "comes"). بدین رزمگاه is an adverbial phrase "to this battlefield" where بدین is an archaic compound meaning "to/at this" and رزمگاه is "battlefield"; the word order (verb before the adverbial) is typical of the poetic register.
segments.s268-g39: نبینی is the negative 2nd-person present/subjunctive/optative of دیدن used idiomatically as a general statement "you will not see / there is not to be seen." ز introduces the source "from"; هر دو سپه means "both armies/hosts;" کس is an indefinite "any person/anyone"; بپای (here) is a verbal/adjectival form meaning "standing, remaining" (i.e., "anyone left standing"). The order is poetic: object phrases precede the verb.
segments.s268-g40: برفته is a prefixed participial/adjectival form meaning "having gone / departed" and modifies روان "the soul/spirit." تن "the body" is the grammatical subject of بمانده "remains / is left" (past participle/verb phrase). به جای is a fixed locative phrase "in its place"; together the clause says "the soul having gone, the body remains in its place."
segments.s268-g81: وز آن‌سو functions as a poetic spatial prepositional phrase ('on/from that side'), where وز is an archaic variant of از; که introduces a subordinate clause. لهراسب شد is a past event: لهراسب (subject) + شد (past 'became' / copula) and جنگجوی is the predicate nominative (a complement meaning 'warrior') following the verb.
segments.s268-g82: الانان و غر are coordinated direct objects (two items being listed). در is the preposition 'into/in' that combines with the verb سپارم ('I will entrust / I entrust'); سپارم is first-person present/subjunctive form expressing the speaker's intention. بدوی here is an immediately following modifier/particle (archaic/poetic) whose precise meaning is uncertain in isolation; the core syntax is object(s) + preposition + verb 'I entrust'.
segments.s268-g83: از این مرز ... تا کوه قاف is a range-expression: از ... تا marks the extent 'from this border up to Mount Qāf'. پیوسته ('continuously/without break') qualifies the entire span; the word order places the extent phrase before the verb that will govern it.
segments.s268-g84: به خسرو سپاریم: به introduces the beneficiary ('to/for Khosrow') and سپاریم is the verb (first-person plural/present-subjunctive or a hortative use meaning 'I/we will entrust' or 'let us entrust'); the following adverbial phrase بی‌جنگ و لاف ('without war and boasting') modifies the manner of entrusting. Word order puts beneficiary + verb + manner phrase in typical poetic position.
segments.s268-g85: وز آن‌سو که introduces another subordinate clause ('and on that side where ...'). اشکش is a noun with a possessive clitic meaning 'its/his/her tear' and بشد here is a past/resultative sense ('it happened / it was made / it fell' — i.e. 'its tear was shed'). همچنین is an adverb 'likewise / in the same way' connecting this clause to what follows.
segments.s268-g86: بپردازم is a first‑person (singular) present/subjunctive form of پرداختن used here as a volitional/future-like verb 'I will give/hand over / I will expend'; اکنون is a temporal adverb 'now'; سراسر زمین is the direct object phrase 'the whole land / throughout the earth'. The verb precedes its direct object and is modified by the temporal adverb.
segments.s268-g87: وز آن پس که introduces a temporal clause 'and after that, when ...'. این کرده باشم is a perfect-subjunctive construction: کرده (the past participle 'done') + باشم (subjunctive/auxiliary 'be') yields 'when I shall have done this / when I have done this.' همه follows as a generalizing element 'all / everyone' that will be picked up by the main clause.
segments.s268-g88: ز هر سو 'from every side' is the source phrase; بر خویش is a dative/benefactive expression ('to/for himself / to myself' — here the clitic ش attaches to خوی to form خویش, 'one's own'); خوانم is the first‑person present/future-like verb 'I will call/summon' (خواند#خوان sense 'to call/summon'), and رمه is the direct object 'flock(s)'. The sequence yields 'I will summon flocks to myself/from every side.'
segments.s268-g169: This short clause names the person presented to the assembly. پسر بد ('the bad/wicked son') is a noun + postpositive adjective (typical Persian word order: noun then adjective). مر is an archaic/dialectal datival/clitic form related to مرا/مرا (here functioning as a topical/dative element), او را is the definite object marked by را, and سر انجمن is a genitival/nominal phrase 'head/chief of the assembly' (سر = chief/head; انجمن = assembly). The clause is elliptical (the main verb is omitted), a common poetic device: it effectively says 'they put/brought the wicked son before the assembly.'
segments.s268-g170: یکی نام ... introduces an appositive identifying that son. یکی ('one, a certain one') introduces the individual; نام here functions as 'named'/'by the name' (i.e. 'one whose name is'); رویین and رویینه تن are epithets: رویین 'iron‑hard/iron-faced' and رویینه + تن 'iron-bodied' (تن = body/person). The coordinating و simply links the two epithets.
segments.s268-g171: بدو گفت is a common classical formula meaning 'he said to him' (بدو = to him/unto it; گفت here stands for 'said' though the supplied sense list is nominal). The following phrase is an imperative address: نزدیک گودرز شو = 'become near, Goudarz' i.e. 'come closer, Goudarz' (نزدیک + vocative name + شو 'be (imperative)').
segments.s268-g172: This line contains two imperatives in apposition. سخن گوی is an imperative clause 'speak (the) speech' (سخن = speech/words, گوی here functions as 'say/speak' or 'sayer' depending on meter), with هشیار as a vocative epithet 'O wise/alert one.' و links the next imperative: پاسخ شنو = 'hear the answer' (پاسخ = answer; شنو = listen/hear, imperative).
segments.s268-g173: چو is the conjunction 'when'; it introduces a temporal subordinate clause. رویین is the subject (here a proper/name-like epithet). برفت is the past verb 'went forth/left' (classical past), and از در نامور is a prepositional phrase 'from the gate/door of Namavar' (از = from; در = gate/door; نامور = proper name/adjective 'renowned').
segments.s268-g174: This nominal phrase describes who went: فرستاده 'a messenger' (subject/agent) followed by با ده سوار دگر 'with ten other horsemen' (با = with; ده سوار = ten riders/horsemen; دگر = other/more). Word order is noun + adjuncts, typical for Persian.
segments.s268-g175: بیامد is the past tense 'came.' The following two words are epithets in apposition to the subject: خردمند 'wise/sensible' and روشن‌روان 'bright‑souled/clear‑spirited' (both function as adjectival/nominal descriptors of the arriving person).
segments.s268-g176: This phrase gives the destination/limit reached: دمان (an older spelling/variant of دامن 'hem, skirt') used metaphorically for 'the edge/threshold'; تا = 'as far as/to'; سراپرده پهلوان = 'the hero's inner chamber/behind‑the‑curtain room' (سراپرده = inner apartment/curtain area; پهلوان = hero/champion). Together they mean he came up to the hero's inner chamber/threshold.
segments.s268-g185: ز (here = از) is the poetic preposition 'from/about' introducing the source of questioning. پیران is the plural noun 'elders' (the object of asking). بپرسید is a past‑narrative 3rd‑person singular form 'he asked' (not an imperative here). وز لشکرش: وز is a poetic clitic combining و + از (here functioning like 'and about'), linking the following noun to the question; لشکرش is 'his army' (لشکر + enclitic possessive ش). The whole segment therefore means 'he asked the elders about his army.'
segments.s268-g186: This is a rhythmic list introduced again by ز (= از, 'about') and repeated linking clitics وز (= و + از) between items. گردان here means 'troop/brigade', شاه 'king', کشورش 'his country/realm' (کشور + enclitic ش). Word order is a simple coordinated list of objects of 'asked' from the previous clause.
segments.s268-g187: خردمند رویین is a noun phrase 'the (iron‑)hard/stalwart wise man' with adjective after the noun (poetic order). پس is a temporal adverb 'then'. آن نامه پیش places the definite object 'that letter' before the verb; پیش here is an adverb meaning 'forward, before (in the presence of [someone])' and sets up the forthcoming verb.
segments.s268-g188: بیاورد is a 3rd‑person past narrative form 'he brought' (here carrying 'that letter' into view). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. بگزارد is a transitive verb meaning 'to place/put/submit' (here 'to present/deliver'); it continues the past narration. پیغام خویش is 'his message'—the possessive is split: خوی (root 'own/self' in this construction) plus the enclitic ش marking 'his'.
segments.s268-g189: دبیر آمد is a simple past clause 'the secretary came' (دبیر subject + آمد past 3rd sg). و connects the next action. نامه بر خواند (برخواند written as one word) is a past verb 'read aloud' or 'recited' with نامه as its object; زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' modifying برخواند.
segments.s268-g190: به گودرز گفت is a ditransitive construction 'he told Goudarz' (به + name marks the indirect object). آنچ در نامه بود is a content clause 'what had been in the letter' (آنچ = 'that which', در نامه = 'in the letter', بود = past copula 'was'). Together the clause reports the content the secretary communicated to Goudarz.
segments.s268-g191: چو is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'. نامه به گودرز is the direct object with the prepositional phrase 'to Goudarz' (به گودرز). بر خواندند is past tense 3rd‑person plural 'they read aloud' (the plural ending -ند marks the plural subjects understood from context). The clause sets the time for the reaction in the next segment.
segments.s268-g192: همه نامداران is the subject 'all the renowned/illustrious ones'. فرو is an adverb/verb prefix indicating downward or intensive effect; combined with ماندند (past 3rd‑person plural of ماندن 'to remain/ be left'), فرو ماندند is an idiomatic past construction meaning 'they were brought low / they remained down' — i.e. they were stunned, struck dumb, or abashed. The verb is plural to agree with همه نامداران.
segments.s268-g153: وگر is the conditional 'if' (contrastive 'but if'); من شوم is first‑person subjunctive/present ('I become' / 'may I become') with the subject before the verb. بر تو is the prepositional phrase 'over/against you' and پیروزگر is a predicative noun 'victor/victorious one' — together: 'if I become victorious over you.'
segments.s268-g154: دهد is an optative/jussive third‑person singular ('may [it/one] give'); مر functions as a dative 'to me'. را is the object marker attached before اختر نیک (the subject/agent in this poetic order), and بر here locates/relates the gift to the preceding clause ('upon/for'). The clause is a classical inversion: 'may a good star give to me [this].' (Some word‑breaks are poetic/archaic.)
segments.s268-g155: نسازم is ن‑ (negation) + سازم (1st‑person present/subjunctive of ساختن) 'I will not make/set'. به ایرانیان is the indirect object 'for/to the Iranians' and بر کمین is the idiomatic phrase 'on/into ambush' (کمین ساختن = to prepare an ambush).
segments.s268-g156: نگیریم is the negative present/subjunctive/plural 'we will not take/be seized by' (here 'take' as in 'take on anger'). خشم is 'anger'. و links the clauses. نجوییم is the negative subjunctive/plural of جستن 'to seek/search' (here 'to seek vengeance'). کین is 'vengeance/hatred.'
segments.s268-g157: سوی شهر ایران is a prepositional phrase 'toward the city of Iran' placed before the verb. دهم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive of دادن ('I give' / 'I will give' / 'I will lead'), and راهشان 'their way/the road for them' is the object — together: 'I will give/lead them the way toward the city of Iran.'
segments.s268-g158: گذارم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive of گذاشتن/گذاردن 'I will place/send/let pass'. یکایک is an adverb 'one by one / each and every.' سوی شاهشان 'toward their king' completes the action: 'I will send/place them, one by one, before their king.'
segments.s268-g159: This is a classical negative existential construction with inverted word order: از ایشان 'from them' + نگردد (present/subjunctive negative of گشتن 'to become/happen') + یکی 'one' + کاسته (past participle 'diminished'). Literally: 'From them will not happen that one is diminished' → 'Not one will be lost/diminished from them.'
segments.s268-g160: شوند is the plural subjunctive/auxiliary 'they become/will be.' ایمن از جان 'safe from life' idiomatically means 'safe in person / from death' or 'secure in life.' وز here functions as an archaic fusion of و + ز 'and from', linking خواسته 'wish/desire' — so 'safe from death and from want/desire.'
segments.s268-g89: به + سوگند + پیمان is an instrumental phrase: به سوگند پیمان کن(م) means 'make a pledge by oath.' کنم is first‑person singular present (here a performative: 'I swear / I will swear'). پیش تو places the action 'in your presence' (prepositional phrase after the verb in this poetic order).
segments.s268-g90: کز introduces a subordinate content clause 'that…'. این پس functions as the temporal phrase 'from now on / henceforth.' نباشم is the negative present/subjunctive form 'I shall not be' (first person), and بداندیش تو is a predicative phrase 'ill‑thinking toward you' (بداندیش describing the speaker's attitude toward تو).
segments.s268-g91: بدانی here is a verb in the optative/subjunctive used as an address 'know (that)…' introducing the report. که is the subordinating 'that', and ما راستی خواستیم is a compact clause 'we wished/desire(d) truth/uprightness' with راستی as the direct object andخواستیم as the past plural verb.
segments.s268-g92: به مهر و وفا is an instrumental phrase 'by/with love and fidelity' (به marking the means). دل is the direct object 'the heart(s)', and بیاراستیم is the first‑person plural past verb 'we adorned' (poetic form of آراستن), meaning they made the heart beautiful/ordered it morally.
segments.s268-g93: سوی + شاه ترکان is the indirect object 'to the king of the Turks' (سوی = toward/to). فرستم is a first‑person present/future 'I send / I will send', and خبر is the direct object 'news/word'. The order is typical of classical Persian poetic compression.
segments.s268-g94: که introduces the reported content 'that…'. ما را uses را as the definite/object marker 'us' as object. ز کینه is an ablative/causal phrase 'out of hatred', بپیچید is past tense 'turned/turned away' (3rd person form with poetic b‑), and سر 'head' is literal here (idiomatically 'they turned their heads' = 'turned away / treated us hostilely').
segments.s268-g95: همیدون is a vocative proper name. تو is the addressed 'you' (emphatic/vocative). نزدیک خسرو is an instruction 'be near / approach Khosrow' and به مهر qualifies the manner 'with kindness/affection' (به marking instrument/manner).
segments.s268-g96: یکی نامه بنویس is an imperative sequence: یکی 'one/a certain', نامه 'letter', بنویس 'write!'. و بنمای چهر is a second imperative clause 'and show/reveal your face' (بنمای = show!, چهر = face/appearance), i.e. present yourself after sending the letter.
segments.s268-g193: ز بس introduces a cause/quantity: ز (preposition 'from/because of') + بس ('so much, great amount'). چرب‌گفتار is a compound noun/adjective 'smooth‑spoken / flattering'. وز here is a poetic connector equivalent to و (and) linking two causes (its lexical senses in the metadata don't match this function). پند خوب is a noun phrase 'good advice' with the adjective following the noun, a normal Persian order.
segments.s268-g194: نمودن is the verb 'to show' used here as the past action (classic poetic usage renders the bare stem) and takes بدو as the indirect object 'to him/unto him'. راه و پیوند (path and bond) are the coordinated direct objects; خوب after them qualifies both as 'good'. The coordinating و links the two nouns.
segments.s268-g195: خردمند پیران is a nominal phrase 'the wise elders' (adjective before noun). که introduces a subordinate/question clause. در نامه یاد is a prepositional phrase 'in the letter (a) mention/remembrance' where یاد functions as 'mention/what was recalled' inside the letter.
segments.s268-g196: The clause is formed with two parallel wh-questions: چه آورد 'what did (they) relate/put' and (وز) پند نیکو چه داد 'and what good counsel did (they) give'. آورد is used in the sense 'relate/report' about the letter's contents; پند نیکو is 'good advice'; داد is the past 'gave'. The token وز functions here as a conjunction (poetic variant of و) linking the two parts — its provided lexical senses do not match that function, so it is treated as a connective.
segments.s268-g197: به رویین is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'with an iron (stern) face' placed before the verb for emphasis; چنین گفت is 'said thus/said so' (گفت is the past verb 'said' — here the metadata's noun senses don't reflect the verbal use). پس is a temporal adverb 'then/after that', and پهلوان is the subject/vocative 'the champion' (classical word order allows verb before subject).
segments.s268-g198: که‌ای functions as an interrogative/vocative phrase equivalent to 'who are you' or the address 'O —' (here introducing the direct address/question). پور سالار و فرخ جوان is a vocative apposition 'son of Salar and fortunate young man' where پور = 'son', سالار a family/title name, فرخ an epithet 'fortunate', and جوان 'young/young man' (adjective or noun as usual in epithets).
segments.s268-g199: تو مهمان ما بود is a copular clause 'you were/are our guest' with بود used as the past copula (classical usage can have a present/epistolary sense). باید is the modal 'must/ought', and نخست is the adverb 'first' indicating the order of what follows.
segments.s268-g200: پس این پاسخ‌نامه بایدت جست: پس 'then'; این پاسخ‌نامه 'this reply/answer-letter'; بایدت is the modal 'it is necessary for you' with the enclitic -ت addressing the second person; جست is the classical form of the verb 'to seek' (here following بایدt to form 'you must seek'). The verb comes after بایدت, as is common in older Persian.
segments.s268-g129: This is an inverted, compressed clause: گنهکار (predicate) + دانی (2nd-person present 'you consider') + مرا را (object 'me') + بی‌گناه (contrastive adjective 'innocent'). In classical Persian the verb دانی takes the object مرا را; the adjective گنهکار precedes the verb for emphasis. The final بی‌گناه is placed as a contrasting predicate ('innocent') that the speaker denies will be assigned by the addressee.
segments.s268-g130: نخواهی is a 2nd-person form meaning 'you will not / you will not be willing' (negative/future sense). The construction به گفتار کردن نگاه is the verb phrase where نگاه کردن ('to look/regard') takes به گفتار ('by/with speech') as an adjunct: literally 'you will not, by speech/utterance, look upon/regard (me)'. In other words, you will not make me innocent by mere words.
segments.s268-g131: کجا begins a rhetorical question 'where'. داد و بیداد is a coordinated pair 'justice and injustice'. نزدت is the preposition نزد with the 2nd-person enclitic ت ('to/for you'), meaning 'in your sight/with you'. یکیست (archaic fused form of یکی است) is a nominal predicate 'is one / are the same'. The clause asks rhetorically whether you treat justice and injustice alike.
segments.s268-g132: جز از ... نیست is an excluding construction: جز 'except', از = 'from/of'. کینه گستردنت is a noun phrase formed with the verbal noun گستردن ('to spread') plus the 2nd-person suffix ت, i.e. 'your spreading of (a) grudge/hatred'. رای نیست is 'there is no counsel/opinion' — here رای means 'plan/counsel'. Together: 'There is no counsel except from (the) spreading of your grudge.'
segments.s268-g133: گزین کن is an imperative formed with the verb stem گزین plus the light verb کن ('do!') giving a forceful command 'choose!'. ز گردان ایران is a prepositional phrase 'from the troop/host of Iran'. سران is the object 'leaders'. Word order (V + PP + object) is compact and typical of epic poetry.
segments.s268-g134: کسی is the indefinite pronoun 'someone/anyone'. کو here is an archaic relative/colloquial marker meaning 'who' (classical 'که'), introducing the relative clause. گراید is a 3rd-person present of گراییدن 'turn/take up' used here as 'takes up' or 'bends to' (i.e. takes up a weapon). به گرز گران is an instrumental/benefactive phrase 'with/to the heavy mace'—meaning one who wields the heavy mace.
segments.s268-g135: This line opens with a proper name همیدون followed by the pronoun من ('I'), then the prepositional phrase از لشکر خویش ('from his/own army') and the noun مرد ('a man'). Because the main verb appears in the next segment, these words function as subject and object/prepositional phrase anticipating 'I will choose a man from the army.' خویش is formed from خوی + ش (possession clitic): خوی here denotes 'own/kin', and ش marks possessive ('his/own').
segments.s268-g136: گزینم is 1st-person singular present/future 'I choose / I will choose' (here future sense: 'I will choose'). چو باید is a temporal/conditional phrase 'when/as it is necessary' (چو = 'when/as' ; باید = 'must/necessary'). ز بهر نبرد is a purpose phrase 'for the sake of battle' (ز بهر = 'for/for the sake of', نبرد = 'battle'). Altogether: 'I will choose ... when required for battle.'
segments.s268-g9: This is a conditional protasis introduced by 'اگر' (if). 'تو' is the 2nd-person addressee, and the relative 'که' links 'تو' to the epithet 'گودرزی' (addressing him as a Gudarz/descendant of Gudarz). 'آن خواستی' is the past-tense second-person verb 'you wished/desired' with the demonstrative/particle 'آن' emphasizing the subordinate clause that follows.
segments.s268-g10: 'که' begins the content clause ("that …"). 'گیتی' is the subject 'the world'. 'به کینه' is a prepositional phrase marking the manner/quality ('with/into hatred'), and the final verb 'بیاراستی' is an archaic/poetic past-form meaning 'you adorned/arranged' (here: you would have made the world full of hatred).
segments.s268-g11: Verb-first order 'برآمد' (arose/issued forth) is followed by the source phrase 'از این کینه' (from this hatred). 'گه' (= 'at times' / 'a time') modifies 'کام تو' (your desire), so the phrase literally says 'from this hatred there arose, at times, your desire.'
segments.s268-g12: Two short interrogative phrases in succession: 'چه گویی' (literally 'what do you say?') is a rhetorical question, and 'چه باشد سرانجام تو' uses the subjunctive/optative 'باشد' to ask 'what will be your outcome/end?'. The repetition of 'چه' stresses the interrogative force.
segments.s268-g13: 'نگه کن' is an imperative ("look!/see!") formed from the noun/verb 'نگه' plus the imperative 'کن'. The complement clause introduced by 'که' describes what to observe: 'چندان دلیران من' = 'so many / such a number of my brave ones' (note that 'من' here marks possession: 'my').
segments.s268-g14: 'ز' (poetic form of 'از') means 'from' or 'among' here; 'خویشان نزدیک' = 'close relatives/near kin', and 'و شیران من' = 'and my lions' (metaphorical: my valiant men). The phrase lists the groups being pointed to by the imperative 'نگه کن'.
segments.s268-g15: The noun phrase 'تن بی سرانشان' = 'their bodies without heads' (literally 'body without their heads') uses the adjective/phrase 'بی سر' (headless) attached to the possessed plural 'ـشان'. The verb 'فگندی' is an archaic/poetic past form (here meaning 'you cast/threw') and is followed by the goal 'به خاک' ('to the earth'). The verb appears after the noun phrase, a common poetic word order.
segments.s268-g16: Prepositional 'ز یزدان' ('of/from God') is fronted; the main predicate 'نداری' is the 2nd-person present negative 'you do not have' (here: 'you have no'). 'همی' is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('at all/indeed'), and 'شرم و باک' are the direct objects 'shame and fear' — together: 'you have no shame or fear of God.'
segments.s268-g137: همه (subject) + یک به دیگر (reciprocal phrase 'one to another') precedes the verb; فراز is an adverbial/Nominal adjunct meaning 'on high/aloft', and آوریم is 1st person plural present/subjunctive ('we bring/let us bring' — here an optative/intentional sense: 'let us / we will bring'). Word order (topic → reciprocal → adverb → verb) is typical of classical Persian poetic style.
segments.s268-g138: سران را: را marks 'سران' as the definite/direct object ('the chiefs' / 'the leaders'). ز سر is a genitive/agentive phrase 'from the head' (here implying removal/beheading or literally 'from the top/head'); سوی = 'toward'. آوریم again is 1pl and repeats the action 'we bring/we will cause to come' so the whole clause says they will bring the leaders' heads forward toward an attack.
segments.s268-g139: همیدون is a proper name placed before the pronouns (vocative or topic). من و تو are the coordinated subjects 'I and you'. به آوردگاه is a prepositional phrase 'to the battlefield/venue of fighting' marking destination. Word order (name → pronouns → to‑phrase) reflects classical poetic ordering.
segments.s268-g140: بگردیم is 1st person plural of گشتن used here with an optative/intentional sense 'we will go/turn/meet/engage'. یک با دگر is a reciprocal/contrastive phrase 'one with the other' (here functioning as 'each against the other' in context). کینه‌خواه is a predicative noun/adjective 'vengeful, avenger' describing the intended stance of the parties.
segments.s268-g141: مگر here introduces an exception or concessive rhetorical clause ('unless' / 'except'). بیگناهان is the plural subject 'the innocent'. ز خون ریختن is a prepositional + verbal-noun phrase meaning 'by/through the shedding of blood' (ز + infinitive/masdar marks cause or means).
segments.s268-g142: به آسایش آیند: به + noun 'to/into ease/peace' + آیند (3rd person plural present/subjunctive of آمدن) 'they come/they may come' — here 'attain peace'. ز آویختن repeats the 'by/through' phrase with the infinitive 'آویختن' ('hanging' or 'being hanged') indicating another means or cause.
segments.s268-g143: کسی is the indefinite 'someone/anyone'. کش here functions as an agentive element attached to a noun (forming 'killer' or 'one who strikes'); in this construction the phrase reads as 'a someone‑killer of the sinner' i.e. 'someone who kills the guilty' (گنهکار modifies the killed/target). داری is 2nd person singular present 'you have/you keep', and همی is an archaic particle giving ongoing/emphatic aspect ('do/indeed/always').
segments.s268-g144: وز (poetic variant of از/or a connective) + او = 'because of him / from him'. بر is the preposition 'on/upon', and دل‌آزار is a compound noun/adjective 'heart‑afflicting / that which troubles the heart'. داری همی repeats 'you have' with the emphatic/continuous همی. The clause says: 'and because of him you bear a heart‑troubling (grief)'.
segments.s268-g41: Literally 'from that after that he-knows that victor who-is'. از آن پس (‘from that time/after that’) is a temporal phrase (از + demonstrative آن + پس). که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/when') and داند is the archaic/poetic 3rd-person singular present of دانستن (‘he/one knows’). The inner clause که پیروز کیست is an embedded question (‘who is the victor’) with پیروز used as a noun (‘victor’) and کیست the copula + interrogative (کی ‘who’ + ست ‘is’).
segments.s268-g42: This clause begins with نگون‌بخت‌گر, where گر is the conditional/optative suffix meaning 'if' (so 'if the ill-fated one...'). گیتی افروز is a two-word compound meaning 'world-illuminator' (literally 'that which lights the world') and functions as the complement of the interrogative clause گیتی افروز کیست (‘who is the world's illuminator?’). کیست is again interrogative/copula.
segments.s268-g43: ور is an archaic conditional particle 'if'. ایدونک is a poetic/archaic temporal/intensifying particle meaning roughly 'even now' or 'at this moment'. پیکار و خون ریختن strings two nouns with an infinitive: پیکار (battle) and خون ریختن (literally 'to pour/spill blood' = 'to shed blood'); here these are verbal-nominal complements listing the actions envisaged under the condition.
segments.s268-g44: بدین رزمگه is a locative phrase formed from به + دین (here functioning as 'this' in poetic register) = 'in/to this', plus رزمگه 'battlefield'. با من آویختن is an infinitival phrase 'to cling to / join with me' (آویختن used figuratively = to join/engage). The whole segment completes the conditional: 'to join me in this battlefield.'
segments.s268-g45: کزین سان is a fixed poetic phrase 'from this way/manner' (کزین = 'from this', سان = 'so/thus'). همی before a verb is an older epic marker that gives a sense of continuing/habitual or future action. جنگ شیران کنی literally 'you do battle of/with the lions' where شیران often functions metaphorically as 'brave men' (heroes); کنی is 2nd-person singular present/epic aorist of کردن used here to mean 'you will make / you will wage'.
segments.s268-g46: همی again marks habitual/future action. از پی is a compound meaning 'in pursuit of/after' (از + پی), شهر ایران 'the city of Iran' is the object, and کنی is the same 2nd-person verbal form 'you will do/you will pursue' — so the clause means 'you will go after (pursue) the city of Iran.'
segments.s268-g47: بگو is the imperative 'say/tell'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that' / 'in order that'). The remainder of this segment (من اکنون هم اندر شتاب) is the subject and adverbial material: من 'I' (nominative), اکنون هم 'even now / also now', اندر شتاب 'in haste' (اندر = in; شتاب = haste). Together they set up the agent and immediacy of the following sending.
segments.s268-g48: نوندی is here a proper name (the person to be sent). فرستم is the 1st-person singular present/subjunctive of فرستادن used in purpose clauses after تا, functioning like 'that I may send' or 'I will send'. به افراسیاب marks the destination/recipient 'to Afrasiab'.
segments.s268-g49: بدان is an imperative formed from ب + دان (= «know/announce»), here used as a command: “tell/let it be known.” The first تا introduces a subordinate/purposive clause (»that/so that«) and بفرمایدم is an archaic/poetic first‑person form meaning roughly “I shall order / I will command.” The second تا begins the content of that command and is followed by زمین (“the land/territory”) as the object.
segments.s268-g50: ببخشیم is a 1st person plural present/subjunctive (with the verbal prefix ب-) meaning “we give/cede” or “we forgive/award” (here: cede/give up land). و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” پس is an adverb “then/after that.” درنوردیم is 1pl with the b‑prefix (subjunctive/immediate future sense) meaning “we will overrun/overcome.” کین is the object, “enmity/vengeance.”
segments.s268-g51: چنانچون is a simile particle meaning “just as / as.” به گاه is a prepositional phrase meaning “at the time of / in the days of” (به + گاه = ‘at the time’). منوچهر شاه is a name with its title in apposition: “Manuchehr, the king.” Word order places the time phrase before the person it modifies.
segments.s268-g52: به بخشش is a prepositional phrase literally “toward/by generosity” (i.e., with regard to generosity). همی is an older persistent/continuous particle (marks ongoing/habitual aspect). داشت is the past/imperfect auxiliary meaning “kept / was holding” (here marking habitual past: “kept/used to keep”). گیتی نگاه is the noun phrase “the world’s gaze / the world’s outlook” (گیتی = world; نگاه = gaze/view); together: “the world kept its gaze on generosity.”
segments.s268-g53: هر آن شهر is a noun phrase “every such city.” کز introduces a relative clause (“which/that” = که + از in contracted poetic form) followed by مرز ایران “the border/frontier of Iran.” نهی here is an archaic/poetic verb in this context meaning roughly “lies / is situated” (classical usage); it forms the predicate of the relative clause describing which cities are meant.
segments.s268-g54: بگو is the imperative “say / proclaim.” The conjunction تا introduces a purpose/result clause “so that / in order that.” کنیم is 1pl subjunctive/future “we may do / we shall make.” آن refers back to «that city/it.» ز is the poetic preposition “of/from,” so ز ترکان تهی literally “empty of the Turks” (تهی is a predicate adjective “devoid/empty of”). The clause therefore means “proclaim so that we make it empty of Turks.”
segments.s268-g55: وز here is a poetic contraction (و + از) used to link items (“and of…”). آباد و ویران is a correlative pair “prosperous (inhabited) and ruined,” common in classical Persian to list opposite conditions of places. هر بوم و بر is a fixed pair meaning “every homeland / dwelling and region” (بوم و بر = land and place). The phrase enumerates all kinds of places, prosperous or desolate.
segments.s268-g56: که introduces a relative clause “which/that.” فرمود is the past tense verb “he commanded/declared/said.” کیخسرو دادگر is the subject in apposition: “Kai Khosrow, the Just” (دادگر = epithet ‘just’). Word order places the verb before its subject inside the relative clause, a normal pattern in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s268-g25: گه آمد که is a temporal/conditional phrase meaning “if/when it comes to pass”; که introduces the subordinate clause. بخشایش آید ترا places the verb آید (present/subjunctive/habitual “comes” or “may come”) before the object pronoun ترا (ت + را), an older/poetic word order: ترا is the 2nd-person object (ت) plus the definite/object marker را.
segments.s268-g26: ز marks source/cause (“from”); کین جستن is a compound noun/infinitive phrase “seeking vengeance.” آسایش آید ترا repeats the verb–object order: آسایش (peace/comfort) is the noun subject/thing that will come, آید is the verb, and ترا is the object pronoun “to you.”
segments.s268-g27: اگر introduces a condition. بازیابی شده is a passive/perfect participial construction meaning “having been restored/recovered” (بازیابی + شده), and روزگار is the nominal subject “the times/fortune”; word order is noun phrase after the conditional particle.
segments.s268-g28: به گیتی is a locative phrase “in/within the world”; درون تخم کینه is a noun phrase “inside the seed/egg of hatred” (درون = inside, تخم = seed/origin, کینه = hatred). مکار here functions as a short, poetic prohibition/advice — effectively “do not be deceitful / do not foster” — addressing the addressee; classical poetry often uses such terse predicates after a locative phrase.
segments.s268-g29: روانت (روان + ت) is the possessive form “your soul” (noun + 2nd-person suffix). مرنجان is a negative imperative “do not anger/grieve” (causative‑like negative command). و links the two commands; مگذار is another negative imperative “do not allow/let,” followed by تن “body,” which is the object of مگذار and connects to the following clause.
segments.s268-g30: ز خون ریختن is a genitive/ablative phrase “from/of blood-shedding” (ز = from, خون ریختن = shedding blood). بازکش is an imperative telling the addressee to withdraw or draw back; it stands before the object خویشتن (emphatic oneself), so the clause means essentially “withdraw yourself from shedding blood.” Note: بازکش here is an archaic/poetic imperative form.
segments.s268-g31: پس از مرگ is a temporal prepositional phrase “after death” (پس = after, از marks the noun). نفرین بود is an existential/predicative construction “there was a curse” (نفرین = curse, بود = was). بر کسی is the prepositional phrase “upon anyone/any person,” completing the predicate.
segments.s268-g32: کز introduces a relative clause “who/that” (کز = که + ز “from/whom”), او = he; نام زشتی is a compound meaning “a bad/ugly name” (نام = name/reputation, زشتی = ugliness/moral vileness). بماند is a verb meaning “remains/is left,” and بسی is an adverb/quantifier adding emphasis (‘much’/‘a great deal’), so the clause means “who leaves behind much bad repute.”
segments.s268-g97: چنین is an adverb placed at the front meaning “thus/so,” setting the tone for the whole statement. از ره is a prepositional phrase ('from the way / with regard to'); مهر و پیکار are two coordinated nouns ('love and combat'). من here is a postposed personal pronoun typical of classical/poetic Persian, marking the speaker (functioning like 'my' or 'I' tied to the phrase). The whole phrase reads literally 'thus, from the way of love and battle of me.'
segments.s268-g98: ز introduces the content/topic ('concerning' or 'about'). خون ریختن is a verbal-nominal construction (خون + infinitive ریختن) functioning as a gerund/nominal phrase 'shedding blood' or 'blood‑letting.' با تو is the prepositional phrase 'with you' and modifies گفتار من ('my speech/talk'); من is again a postposed possessive pronoun ('my') attached to گفتار. Word order (Z + gerund + accompaniment + nominal possessor) is typical of older poetic Persian.
segments.s268-g99: چو here introduces a conditional/subordinate clause ('if' / 'when'). پیمان همه is 'the whole pact' (همه quantifies the pact); کرده باشیم is a compound perfect/subjunctive (past participle + subjunctive of بودن) used in classical Persian to mean 'we have made' or 'if we have made' in the subordinate clause. راست is an adverb 'truly/faithfully' modifying the clause and placed at the end for emphasis.
segments.s268-g100: ز marks source/recipient sense here ('from' / 'of' - i.e. 'asked of me'). من is the indirect object 'me' (postposed). خواسته is a past participle/nominal form meaning 'asked/desired' or 'that has been desired'; هرچ ('whatever') introduces the content of the request. خسرو is the subject and بخواست is the simple past ('he desired/asked'); the clause therefore reports that whatever the king wanted, he asked it of me. Classical word order and participial constructions produce what looks like duplication but is a normal older Persian pattern.
segments.s268-g101: فرستم is first-person singular present/future ('I send' / 'I will send'); in classical Persian the present form often carries future meaning. همه سر به سر is an idiomatic reduplication meaning 'all, one by one / every single one' (literally 'head to head'). نزد شاه is a locative/prepositional phrase 'to the presence of the king' with نزد = 'near/at (the presence of)'. The verb governs the following destination phrase; the implied direct object ('them' / 'every head') is expressed by the idiom.
segments.s268-g102: در + کین = 'in/for vengeance' (کین = 'vengeance, hatred'). ببندد is third-person singular present/subjunctive of بستن ('to bind/close'); the subject is omitted but is the previously mentioned شاه (so: 'that he bind'). مگر here functions idiomatically with بر سپاه to mark direction/target ('against' the army) — classical usage allows مَگر to link to بر + noun; بر سپاه = 'against/upon the army.' The clause therefore expresses purpose/result: sent to the king so that he may bind them in vengeance against the army.
segments.s268-g103: از آن پس که is a temporal connector 'after that / from that time that' introducing a subordinate temporal clause. این کرده باشیم is the same perfect/subjunctive compound as before ('we have done this' / 'once we have done this'); the verb form places the action as completed in the subordinate clause. نیز is the conjunctive adverb 'also/too' linking this clause to what follows.
segments.s268-g104: گروگان فرستاده و داده is a coordination of two past-participial forms used as main predicates in a compact poetic style: 'گروگان فرستاده' = 'hostages (have been) sent' (فرستاده = 'sent'), and 'داده' = 'given.' The omitted subject is 'we' (from the previous clause). چیز ('things') is the broad object of داده, meaning 'things/gifts/matters' depending on context. Word order reflects older Persian terseness: possessor/actor omitted when clear from context.
segments.s268-g113: نباید is an impersonal negative modal meaning 'it should not / must not (be that...)' and introduces the prohibition; کز is the archaic contracted preposition 'from/that from' (here 'from'), so کز این خوب‌گفتار من = 'from these my well-spoken words' (خوب‌گفتار is a compound meaning 'well-spoken / eloquent words' modifying من). The whole phrase is the source clause governed by نباید.
segments.s268-g114: This clause contains the verb برند (3rd person plural present/subjunctive form of بردن, 'they take/harbor') as the main predicate whose action is forbidden by نباید. گمانی ('a supposition') is the object, qualified by the prepositional phrase به سستی ('into/with regard to weakness'), and انجمن ('the assembly') is the (understood) subject performing برند.
segments.s268-g115: که introduces an explanatory subordinate clause ('that / for'), من is the subject and جز marks an exception ('except'). به مهر = 'out of affection / by goodwill' (به as an adverbial preposition meaning 'by / out of'), این is the thing excepted, and نگویم is a present subjunctive/negative first person form 'I do not say' (here used to mean 'I say nothing but...'); همی is an archaic emphatic/continuous particle following the verb.
segments.s268-g116: سرانجام is an adverb 'in the end / ultimately', نیکی is the object 'goodness / a good outcome', and بجویم is first-person present/subjunctive of جستن 'I seek' (here a volitional present: 'I seek / I will seek'); the particle همی again gives habitual/continuous sense 'ever / always'.
segments.s268-g117: مرا here functions as the topical/possessive element 'my' (written as م + را in the segmentation; را is the object/topic marker attached to the possessor), introducing the possessed items 'گنج و مردان' ('treasure and men'). از آن تو بیش literally 'of that more is yours' – از indicates 'of/regarding', تو is the possessor, and بیش 'more' marks the comparative sense: 'more are yours.'
segments.s268-g118: به here introduces a domain ('with respect to / in'), so به مردانگی = 'in bravery/valor'. نام ('name') means 'reputation/fame' and is the topic; از آن تو indicates 'of that (quality) you (have it)', and پیش is an adverb 'before / in preference' meaning 'yours foremost / ahead (of mine)'. The sequence states that the fame for bravery rightly belongs to you in precedence.
segments.s268-g119: ولیکن is the adversative 'but / yet'. بدین is the classical prepositional phrase 'by/with this' (here segmented as ب + دین in the input), introducing the verbal noun phrase کینه انگیختن 'to arouse hatred' (انگیختن is the infinitive 'to stir/rouse'). The clause contrasts the prior disclaimers with the forbidden act of provoking hatred.
segments.s268-g120: به here marks manner ('by / through'), so به بیداد = 'by injustice'; هر جای 'every place' modifies the verb ریختن ('to spill / to shed'), and خون is 'blood' (here 'bloodshed'). The verbal noun construction links back to انگیختن: 'to arouse hatred by unjustly shedding blood everywhere.'
segments.s268-g209: به یک هفته is a temporal prepositional phrase 'after/after the lapse of one week' (classical Persian uses به + time). The proper name گودرز follows that phrase as the sentence's agent. با + رود و می is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with music and wine' where رود (here meaning musical sound/instrument) and می (wine) are conjoined by و.
segments.s268-g210: همی is an archaic/emphatic particle often used before verbs in epic poetry to mark immediate or continuous action. نامه را is the definite direct object (را marks it). پاسخ is the noun 'reply' functioning as the object of افگند (literally 'cast/threw' but here 'sent/dispatched'). افگند is a past action verb 'sent/cast'; پی after the verb means 'after/afterwards, in pursuit' and modifies افگند ('sent after').
segments.s268-g211: ز بالا is a poetic locative 'from above / on high' (ز = 'from'). چو introduces a simile 'like/as', linking to خورشید گیتی فروز which is an epithet 'sun, world‑illuminating' (گیتی followed by the adjective/verb فروز). The adjective/participial sense of فروز 'illuminating' modifies خورشید.
segments.s268-g212: بگشتی is a past tense verb meaning 'returned/turned' (classical word order often places the verb before its subject). سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed / commander'. گه نیم‌روز is an adverbial time phrase 'at the time/occasion of midday' (گه 'occasion/at the time of', نیم‌روز 'noon').
segments.s268-g213: می‌و رود و مجلس بیاراستی is a clause describing how the assembly was prepared: می‌و (classical sequence meaning 'wine and ...') and رود ('music, song' / instrumental sound) are conjoined, and بیاراستی is the verb 'adorned/arranged' (classical past), taking مجلس 'the assembly' as its object. Word order places the instrument/contents before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s268-g214: فرستاده را is the definite object 'the messenger' (را marking definite object). پیش خود is a fixed phrase 'before him / in his presence' (پیش = 'in the presence of', خود = 'himself/his own person'). خواستی is a past tense verb 'desired/wanted' but in context functions as 'had [him] come' or 'summoned'—classical Persian often uses 'خواست' with a meaning close to 'ordered/summoned'.
segments.s268-g215: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. یک هفته بگذشت is a temporal clause 'one week passed' (بگذشت = past of 'to pass' for time). هشتم پگاه is a precise time adverbial 'on the eighth morning / at the eighth dawn'—classical epic often stacks time expressions (first 'a week passed', then the precise morning).
segments.s268-g216: نویسنده را is the definite object 'the scribe' (را marks it). خواند here is the verb 'called/summoned' (one of its classical senses); in this order the verb precedes the subject. سالار شاه is the subject phrase referring to the king as commander/sovereign ('the lordly king' or 'the king, the commander'), so the whole clause reads 'the king summoned the scribe'.
segments.s268-g161: ور is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an archaic emphatic/tempo particle (poetic) meaning roughly 'thus/so/now'. زین سان is a fixed phrase meaning 'in this manner' (زین here functions idiomatically as a demonstrative combined with سان 'like/as'). نجویی is the verbal form from جست‌/جوی 'seek', here 2nd-person singular with an initial negative element (ن‑) built into the surface form, so 'you do not seek'; نبرد is a noun 'battle' governed by the preceding verbal idea 'seek (battle)'.
segments.s268-g162: دگرگونه is an adverb 'otherwise'/'in a different way'. خواهی + کرد is a periphrastic future: خواهی (modal/auxiliary 'you will') + کرد (the verbal stem 'do') yields 'you will do'. همی is a classical intensifier/continuative particle placed before the main verb; کار is the noun 'deed/affair' used here as the direct object of 'do'. Together the clause reads 'you will do a different deed' → 'you will act otherwise.'
segments.s268-g163: به introduces a goal or direction 'to/for' (here 'for the purpose of'). انبوه 'numerous/mass' qualifies the following verbal noun جویی 'seeking/pursuit' (a verbal-noun use meaning 'in pursuit of many'). همی is the classical intensifier/continuative again, and کارزار is the noun 'battle/battlefield' that names what is being sought. The phrase thus means roughly 'in pursuit of many (battles)' or 'to seek battle in great numbers.'
segments.s268-g164: سپه 'the army' is the grammatical object marked by را. سراسر 'throughout/wholly' modifies the following infinitival direction. به جنگ 'into/for war' gives the destination, and اند آر here are verbal elements commanding or causing the troop movement: اند is an older/formal auxiliary 'are/let them be' and آر is a poetic verb form meaning 'put/send' (a hortative/imperative flavor in context). The whole clause therefore orders 'send the army wholly into battle.'
segments.s268-g165: هر آن ... که is a standard relative clause 'every/whichever ... that'. خون 'blood' here denotes 'bloodshed' or 'a drop of blood'. آید is the present/occurrent form 'comes' (used here as 'that comes to be / that is shed'), به + کین 'in/for vengeance', and ریخته is the perfect/passive participle 'poured/spilled'. Together: 'every blood that comes to be spilled in vengeance.'
segments.s268-g166: تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you'. باشی here is the subjunctive/optative form of 'to be' used as a future/resultative 'you shall be'. ب + دان together correspond to the enclitic/compound بدان ('to/at that' or 'be considered so'); دان (to consider/know) gives a sense of being reckoned/held so. گیتی 'the world' is the locative/scene, and آویخته is a participle 'hung' or 'hung up'; the clause reads 'you shall be regarded/hung in that world' — i.e., 'you shall be hanged in that realm' (poetic way of expressing condemnation/accountability).
segments.s268-g167: ببست is a past/perfect verb 'he bound/closed'. از بر here is a prepositional phrase 'from/upon' marking source or accompaniment; نامه‌بر is 'letter-carrier/messenger' (direct object), and بند is 'fetter/bond'. The final را is the object marker attached to بند (focusing the object). The clause means 'he bound the messenger with fetters' or 'he bound the courier.'
segments.s268-g168: بخواند is a polyvalent verb; here it functions as 'he summoned/called' (one of its poetic senses). آن 'that/the' modifies گرانمایه 'esteemed/precious', which qualifies فرزند 'son/child'. The final را is the object marker. The clause therefore reads 'he summoned that noble son.'
segments.s268-g65: This is an adverbial/nominal phrase marking extent and topic. دگر (preceding the name) means 'the other / the remaining' and modifies مولیان (a proper name/group). تا در بدخشان is a poetic expression of extent 'as far as / up to Badakhshan' (تا + locative در + place name); the whole phrase functions as a preposed location/extent/topic.
segments.s268-g66: همینست is a contraction of همین است (copula) meaning 'this is / that is exactly so.' از این پادشاهی نشان is a genitive/source phrase 'a sign/token of this kingship' where از functions poetically as 'of' (possession/source). Word order is copula + complement in a short declarative clause.
segments.s268-g67: فروتر is a comparative/adverbial 'lower / further down' placed before the noun for emphasis; دگر here introduces 'another/other (place).' دشت is the head noun 'plain' and آموی و زم are two place-names attached in a coordinate structure (poetry often omits an explicit ezāfe). The whole segment is a locative statement naming two plains.
segments.s268-g68: که introduces a subordinate (here temporal/conditional) clause 'when / that,' با شهر ختلان is an accompaniment phrase 'with the city of Khotlan,' براید is an archaic/poetic verb meaning 'comes forth / sallies out' (3rd sing.), and برم is a 1st-person optative/subjunctive 'I shall go' (expressing the speaker's intended action). Together the clause gives the time/condition upon which the speaker will go.
segments.s268-g69: چه begins an exclamatory clause ('oh/what'); شگنان is a plural noun 'raiders / destroyers' used as the topical/exclamatory subject; وز here is a poetic prepositional variant (effectively 'from') introducing the place ترمذ (Termez). ویسه is a proper name and گرد is a verbal/adverbial element 'to go about / to range' so the clause has inverted/elliptical word order typical of epic verse.
segments.s268-g70: This is a continuation/predicate that names the objects of movement: بخارا 'Bukhara' coordinated with 'a city' (شهری) qualified by the relative clause که هستش ('which is / that stands there'). بگرد here is a verbal form meaning 'he/it went about / ranged around' (poetic present/preterite sense rather than a literal modern imperative).
segments.s268-g71: همیدون (a place/name) is the destination, and برو is the 2nd-person singular imperative 'go.' تا here introduces purpose/extent ('so that / as far as') and در سغد نیز is a locative phrase 'into Sogd as well' (نیز = also). The sequence is an imperative followed by its extent/purpose clause.
segments.s268-g72: نجوید is a subjunctive/jussive form of 'seek' functioning as a prohibitive/jussive clause here ('let no one seek' / 'may no one seek'), with کس 'anyone' as the indefinite subject. آن پادشاهی is the object 'that kingship/sovereignty' and بنیز is an adverbial particle 'any longer / besides' (poetic). The verb-first order (نجوید کس) is typical of older/epic Persian.
segments.s268-g121: بسوزد is an old/poetic present-jussive form (ب‑ + سوزد) meaning 'may it burn' or 'it burns (continually)'; همی is a durative particle ('still/continually') placed after the verb. The two prepositional phrases بر سپه and بر دلم mark direction/affected locus: بر سپه ('against/toward the host') qualifies the object of the feeling and بر دلم ('on my heart') gives the location. The final م is an archaic clitic (from هست) indicating existence/state ('is' — here tying the feeling to the speaker's heart).
segments.s268-g122: بکوشم is first‑person singular present/jussive with the prefix ب‑ meaning 'I will try / I strive'. که introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result ('so that' / 'that'). کین is the object 'vengeance/hatred', and از میان is a prepositional expression 'from the midst / out of the middle'. بگسلم is a present/jussive verb (ب‑ + گسلم) 'I may split/tear apart' and completes the purpose clause: 'I will try that I may sever this hatred from the midst.'
segments.s268-g123: سه دیگر is a numeral phrase 'three more/three other (things)'; دیگر here means 'other/more'. که begins the following clause. از کردگار جهان is a prepositional phrase 'from/of the Maker (Creator) of the world' where از marks the source/agent that is feared and کردگار جهان is the compound noun 'Creator of the world'.
segments.s268-g124: بترسم is a first‑person (or jussive) present form with ب‑ 'I fear' / 'that I fear' (here part of the subordinate clause). همی again is the durative particle 'always/continually'. آشکار و نهان are coordinated adverbial/ adjectival terms 'openly and secretly' ( آشکار 'openly/manifest', نهان 'hidden/in secret') that qualify the mode of fearing; Persian often places such adverbials after the verb.
segments.s268-g125: که introduces a clause of reason/explanation. نپسندد is third‑person present negative (ن‑ + پسندد) 'will not approve / does not approve'. از ما بدی is the object phrase 'evil from us' (از marking origin). دادگر follows as the subject ('the Just one' / 'the Just ruler' — here a title, often applied to God or a righteous judge), so the literal order is verb + object + subject: 'that the Just will not approve of evil from us.'
segments.s268-g126: گزافه is an adverb/adjective meaning 'trifling / not to be taken lightly / not in vain' (here qualifying the seriousness). نبردارد is a negated present form (ن‑ + بردارد) used idiomatically to mean 'does not carry off / is not to be treated as trivial' — in context it says this thing 'is not without consequence / is not trifling'. این شور و شر is the topicalized subject 'this tumult and mischief' (این demonstrative + two coordinated nouns شور 'tumult/passion' and شر 'evil/trouble'). Grammatically the predicate (نبردارد) precedes the subject, a common poetic order.
segments.s268-g127: اگر is the conditional 'if'. سر here means 'head' and is used in the idiom 'سر بپیچی ز گفتار من' — بپیچی (ب‑ + پیچی) is second‑person subjunctive/imperative 'you turn' / 'you turn away'; پیچی from پیچیدن 'to turn'. ز (poetic variant of از) means 'from', and گفتار من is 'my speech/words' with the possessor من after the noun (typical Persian possessive order).
segments.s268-g128: نجویی is a negative jussive/imperative form (ن‑ + جویی) meaning 'do not seek / do not inquire / do not pry'. همه 'all' and ژرف 'deep/profound' together modify کردار من 'my deeds/conduct' (کردار = deed/conduct, من = my). The object phrase 'همه ژرف کردار من' states what must not be sought; Persian often puts modifiers (همه ژرف) before the head noun کردار.
segments.s268-g17: ز + noun phrase ('مهر و خرد') is a prepositional source 'from / because of'; the coordinated nouns 'مهر و خرد' (love and wisdom) form the complement. 'روی' is the noun 'face' and 'برتافتی' is a poetic 2nd-person past/present‑perfect-type verb form attached to it (literally 'your face appeared/was shown' here best read as 'you turned/your face turned or shone'), so the whole segment means 'your face (turned/was shown) because of love and wisdom.' Word order is Preposition — Nouns — (direct noun + verb).
segments.s268-g18: کنون is an adverb 'now'. آنچ is a relative/indefinite 'that which' (what). جستی here is the verb 'you sought' (sense 'seek'), forming the relative clause 'that which you sought'. همه quantifies the following verb; یافتی is the 2nd‑person past 'you found/obtained' and completes the statement 'now you have found all that you sought.'
segments.s268-g19: گه آمد که is a stock phrase 'it sometimes happens that / there are occasions when' (گه آمد = 'there comes an occasion that'). گردی is a poetic nominal/verbal element indicating a temporary state of the person addressed (here read as a present/occurring state — the listed lexical senses give 'wandering', which in context signals a change/condition), followed by the prepositional phrase 'از این کینه' ('from/with this grudge/hatred') and the adjective/noun 'سیر' meaning 'sated' or 'filled'. Grammatically this segment sets up 'on some occasions you are (in a) state, sated with this hatred.'
segments.s268-g20: به خون ریختن is an infinitival phrase 'to shed/spill blood' (به marks direction/purpose and ریختن is the infinitive 'to pour/spill'); چند is interrogative 'how long / for how long' and باشی is the 2nd‑person verbal form ('you be' — here optative/subjunctive sense 'will you be'), with دلیر the predicate adjective 'brave'. The clause therefore asks rhetorically 'how long will you be brave in shedding blood?'.
segments.s268-g21: نگه کن is an imperative 'look!' or 'take heed!'. کز here functions as a prepositional/subordinating element 'from / of which' introducing origin or source, followed by the genitive phrase 'ایران و توران سوار' ('the riders/horsesmen of Iran and Turan'). Word order is imperative verb + subordinate introducer + origin phrase.
segments.s268-g22: چه مایه is an exclamatory interrogative 'what a ... / what sort of ...'. تبه شد is a compound verb: تبه (ruin/mischief) + شد (has become) meaning 'has become ruinous / a calamity has befallen'. بدین is the adverbial/demonstrative 'by/with this' (here written as the element ب + دین in the segmentation; in the text it functions as 'with/at this') and کارزار is 'battle/battlefield'. The whole clause asks 'what a ruin has befallen this battlefield.'
segments.s268-g23: به کین جستن is an infinitival noun phrase 'to seek vengeance' (به marks purpose/direction and جستن is the infinitive 'to seek'). مرده‌ای ناپدید is a noun phrase 'a dead one (مرده‌ای) who is vanished / missing (ناپدید)'; the object of the infinitive follows it, a common poetic order.
segments.s268-g24: سر زندگان 'heads of the living' — سر (head) is the noun and زندگان ('the living') is in apposition/genitive-like relation. چند is the interrogative 'how many'; باید is the impersonal modal 'it is necessary / must'; برید here is a past/optative form of بریدن in classical usage meaning 'be cut / to cut off' (the available lemma senses do not list 'to cut', but in context برید is the verb 'to sever'). The clause rhetorically asks how many living heads must be severed in pursuit of vengeance for the dead.
segments.s268-g145: به پیش تو: a prepositional phrase placed at the front meaning “before you / in your presence” (به + پیش + pronoun). آرم is a 1st‑person verb form in poetic diction (here a contracted/poetic form of ‘bring’/‘produce’, functioning as ‘I will bring’ or ‘I bring’). به‌روز نبرد is a noun phrase “the day of battle” (به‌روز modifying نبرد) serving as the object/thing being brought before you.
segments.s268-g146: ببایدت is an impersonal/modal construction with the enclitic ‑ت marking obligation for ‘you’ (‘it is necessary for you’ / ‘you must’). پیمان یکی نیز is the direct object: پیمان ‘a covenant’ qualified by یکی ‘one’ and نیز ‘also’. کرد is the lexical verb ‘make/do’ (here functioning with ببایدت to mean ‘you must make’); the verb form is the past stem used in the periphrastic obligation construction common in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s268-g147: که introduces a subordinate/conditional clause (here equivalent to ‘if’/‘should’). بر ما is a prepositional phrase ‘upon us/against us’ that precedes the actor. تو‌گر is the conditional particle + pronoun ‘if you’; دست‌یابی is a deverbal noun ‘attainment/getting hold (of)’, followed by به خون ‘to blood’ (i.e. ‘if you attain to blood / if you draw blood’). The clause thus expresses a conditional event.
segments.s268-g148: شود is a 3rd person singular jussive/subjunctive marker meaning ‘may it be / will become’. بخت is ‘fortune/fate’ as the subject whose state changes; گردان is an adjectival/participial element meaning ‘turned/revolving’ in poetic usage (the idea ‘fortune turned’). ترکان is the group affected (‘the Turks’) and نگون is a predicate adjective ‘ill‑fated/overturned’. Together the clause prays or predicts that the Turks’ luck will be turned ill.
segments.s268-g149: نیازاری is a vocative proper name at the start of the clause (addressing someone). از بن سپاه is a genitive/partitive phrase ‘from the root/foundation of the army’ (از + بن ‘from the root’ + سپاه ‘army’). مرا here is the possessive/object element attached to سپاه (archaic placement: سپاهِ مرا ‘my army’ realized with م + را); را is the object/topic marker attached in this older poetic word-order.
segments.s268-g150: نسوزی is a negative jussive/imperative form meaning ‘do not burn’ or ‘may it not burn’ (poetic/older morphology). بر و بوم و گاه مرا lists what must not be burned: بر ‘on/upon’, بوم ‘homeland/soil’, and گاه ‘dwelling/place’ with مرا again marking possession (‘my land and home’). The coordinating و links the nouns; the clause is a plea not to lay waste to the speaker’s army, land, and home.
segments.s268-g151: گذرشان دهی is a causative/imperative construction: گذرشان ‘their passage/passing’ (noun + pronoun suffix) + دهی ‘you give/allow’ meaning ‘let them pass’ or ‘grant them passage’. تا introduces a purpose or result clause ‘so that / until’, followed by به توران شوند ‘they may become/go to Turan’ (شوند is 3rd plural subjunctive/optative ‘may they become/go’). The whole segment is a request to allow passage toward Turan.
segments.s268-g152: کمین را نسازی is a prohibitive construction: کمین را ‘an ambush’ marked by را, plus نسازی (negative/imperative of ساختن) ‘do not make/prepare’ — ‘do not lay an ambush’. بر ایشان is the prepositional complement ‘against them’ (بر = against, ایشان = them). کمند at the end is a synonym ‘snare/lasso’ serving as an appositive object: ‘do not make an ambush, a snare against them.’
